replies.author: @thefallenfaith1986
replies.text:
This review has strangely made me want to see a sci-fi based TV show that uses this formula. I really loved Farscape, which mixed SciFi and actin-adventure stuff with humor (although the humor in Farscape was different, and better,than Gunn-style humor), and it also had "quirky" humor like GOTG, which is probably why I want a JamesGunn-style SciFi show. 

But you could basically take the formula that CD described:
- 80s popmusic (or 90s alt-rock and grunge, or 70s Motown)
- snarky, sarcastic humor
- underdogs and misfits creswting a closeknit friendgroup
 
Add good looking actors and actresses with enough charisma,and screen presence, and talent, to come across as sympathetic and likeable; find writers good enough to create some epic events;  get costume and set designers who know their craft, get CGI / SFX guys with talent to make the effects look nice, and wrap it up into an hour long series with long form, serialized storytelling, and you'd have a very nice formula for a show.

You know, it reminds me of the early Brosnan Bond adverts. They were BRILLAINT! He just walks on looking all cool and Bondlike and he just says - " Bond....You know the rest..."

replies.author: @MexiFryStains
replies.text: @ianfudo5040 I started disliking him after guardians 2. The first movie was great, with jokes that are actually funny, serious and emotional scenes that are treated with the appropriate gravitas, and complex and interesting art and set design that doesn't look like a neon rainbow vomited all over the screen. Then in the second movie Gunn took all those things that made the first movie interesting and turned it up to 500%. Suddenly no scene could go longer than 2 minutes without a goofy 1-liner and the saturation was tripled giving us the CGI visual diarrhea that became infamous in later movies like quantumania and MoM. A perfect example I think is that Guardians 2 is to Guardians 1, what Love and Thunder is to Ragnarok. 2 directors finding success in their niche, only to get way overconfident and run wild with their dumb ideas to the detriment of the movie

replies.text:
​@MexiFryStains How you read my comment and interpreted it is interesting. Did I say "Every single movie from Iron Man 1 thru Endgame was on the same level of quality and they're all 10/10."? No. No I didn't. When I said perfect, i was talking about the 21 movies altogether heading in the same direction towards Thanos, hence the "they were ALL leading to the same thing. We just didnt know it at the time." 

I'll say this just to be as clear as I can.. Captain Marvel, Ant Man and the Wasp, people say Thor 2 (I think its better than most people do, but its not a on the top half of the 21 by any means), etc, are nowhere close to the quality of Infinity War and Endgame and the other Avengers and top half of the 21. However, they are still very important and add necessary context for the climax in Infinity War and Endgame. Which is why the overall story throughout the 21 movies is perfect, and its unbelievable that they were able to pull it off. 

In real time we saw why its difficult to have a conversation in a comment thread. You dont know me. You read my comment having to insert assumptions which leads to you interpreting my comment wrong. I do this as well. Everyone does unfortunately. It leads to unnecessary arguments which leads to name calling haha Im sure more than likely you won't read my whole comment and thats cool, but i hope you have a good weekend and holidays.

replies.author: @callmelou5397
replies.text:
​@orpheemulemo8053 Don’t take the scene at face value it was deliberately written.
Superman isn’t just another character; he’s one of the most iconic figures in fiction, with decades of legacy. He’s to superheroes what Darth Vader is to Star Wars.
And context matters here: James Gunn has previously said in interviews that he dislikes Superman as a character and aligns more with Lex Luthor’s perspective. When that’s the mindset of the person in charge, scenes like this stop looking like harmless jokes and start looking like intentional disrespect toward a core icon.

replies.author: @SqueakingLion
replies.text:
My goodness. I make a simple statement, and I've drawn out all the trolls who do nothing but hate on the Critical Drinker. I can't help but notice that they never target any of my other comments, but the second Drinker indicates that he loves this comment, I'm suddenly the target of a hate-fest from every troll that hate-comments on all the other comments that Drinker indicates that he loves. 

Why do they watch his videos if they all hate his opinion so much? Why do they go out of their way to attack every popular comment in his videos? Gosh... such a puzzle... what could possibly be the motivation for that? 

Grow up, children. You're nothing but internet trolls, and you're ridiculously obvious about it. People who disagree with a content creator's opinions will typically just make a post saying they disagree (in one form or another), and then never watch that content creator again. After all, why support a content creator with your comments if you hate them? I mean, you all do understand that every comment you make, every downvote you make, every view you add to a video, every second of watch-time you add to a video... that's all supporting the content creator... you know that, right?  

So by all means... flame away, children. Your opinion means nothing to me, and it supports a content creator I like and agree with. Keep on with your petty insults and childish observations and desperate cries for attention. Keep making him more and more popular with your absurd attempts at trying to one-up anyone you disagree with in the slightest way. The Drinker will laugh all the way to the bank, and I'll still continue to enjoy his videos. 

So... anyone else want to add their ultimately useless whine to my comment? Go for it. I'll be happy to roll my eyes and chuckle at your naivete, and simply block and report anyone who gets particularly nasty about it. Have at it, children... who else wants to support the Drinker with their idiocy?

replies.author: @MisterIceRat
replies.text: ​@theworld6710 Maybe that's the case, but what's the message then? That politeness and social etiquette and being good with the little things has no value because even the most degenerate people are capable of _some_ good? Superheroes are supposed to be aspirational: something to look up to and work towards, but now they've been torn down to the level of the cynical, antisocial people writing them. The same thing has been done to Starfleet officers in Trek and the Doctor in Doctor Who. It's exhausting seeing the same "reluctant, unqualified hero with an unheroic outlook and an unscrupulous history" trope done a hundred million times without a break. This anti-trope only works if there's a good number of optimistic, idealistic, unreluctant, qualified heroes to balance it out. Trek managed to make 5 shows where even these "boring", "goody-two shoes" heroes face interesting moral quandaries and tests of character. In a time where our society is searching for a higher standard of morality, Hollywood had the opportunity to provide it, but instead they're stuck in this 2010s mindset that a higher moral standard is something that's ingenuine, unrealistic, campy, outdated and even discriminatory.

replies.author: @PhilosophicalCat
replies.text: @augustusfreeman4032 The budget was $225 million and the marketing was $100-200 million, putting the total budget at $325-425 million. However, due to the cut the theaters take, a movie needs to make 2.5-3 times its budget to break even. So even if we take the most conservative values, 325 million x 2.5 = $812.5 million just to break even. Superman made roughly $579 million globally and is therefore a flop.

replies.author: @Noodles.Doodles
replies.text:
@HUNTER-v3c  I can't post URLs, but you can google these quotes.
Gunn to Rolling Stone: 
    “I really understand Lex. I feel like I relate to Lex way more than I wish I did. But for me, Lex looks at Superman like artists look at AI. He is the world’s greatest man in so many ways. He’s done these unparalleled things. And then you got a guy who comes in who’s done nothing to deserve the ability to fly and to smash down buildings. And he’s also extraordinarily handsome, too. And all of a sudden, that’s all the world is talking about. And that sort of obsession with being replaced, with being—with your gifts not being seen or passed over — I think is what drives Lex. I relate to everything he does. He’s just meaner than I am.”

Gunn on the Ringer-Verse podcast:
    “He, as a character, is really interesting. There’s something, despite everything, that is incredibly heroic about Lex. Take aside morality… here is this guy who is saying, ‘You can hold up a building. you can shoot down planes with your eyes… I’m going to kick your ass because I’m better than you.’ I can’t help but admire his tenacity and his ego… He’s the underdog… He’s a guy, and I f***ing love that.”

Gunn on the sequel: "It’s as much a Lex movie as it is a Superman movie.”, "And it is Lex’s story too, so it’s the two of them.”

I admit that there's no quote saying 'I don't like Superman', so I shouldn't have said that. However, it would be stupid to say it outright. He does say "There’s a lot of things I love about the character of Superman", "his basic human goodness and kindness", "We all want to be Superman". However, this is entirely detached and generic. None of it gushes with personal, effusive, detailed admiration, the way he talks about Lex. He is making Lex work with Superman as 'co-protagonist' of the sequel. The quotes above, the strangely sadistic depiction of Superman in his own movie, and the Supergirl newspaper shot, combine to paint a picture that I doubt Gunn will ever publicly acknowledge.

replies.author: @GideonRavenor712
replies.text:
Yep.

This has been a common trend I've been noticing with the writing end.

The Studios these days tend to be hiring on writers with NOTHING to their resumes, nothing that would explain how they got onto a million-dollar production.

There's reasons for this beyond ideology: If the writer is a complete noobie, with no reputation in the business of any note, they will be cheaper because they can't demand as much in pay, and they will not be defiant to the studio, Director, Producer, or anyone in charge because they are looking to please their bosses on their first big project.

Same goes for Directors who have no real background to speak of, as we've seen in various shows and other films. Though in this case that doesn't apply to Gunn.

But that's exactly what it looks like, they got a no-name writer with nothing to her resume, to basically write Gunn's style without his name on it.

Also it's painfully clear to anyone who actually knows the source material for this movie (the comic book) that Drinker has absolutely no idea what he's talking about since pretty much all of his criticism is blatantly wrong, assuming the movie is faithful to said source material (which, by the looks of the trailer, it is). Rather than value the judgement of some guy on the internet (who is clearly pushing an agenda), maybe consider forming your own judgements; even a quick 5-minute read of the comic book's synopsis on Wikipedia is enough to debunk this entire video.

replies.author: @Edward-op3yc
replies.text:
​@twixraiderhmYou could be more right than wrong about CD and his analysis. I am not much of a fan of DC comics so I can't argue about source material. I think the alcohol , depressive, teenager thing is overused - way overused. Would it be possible to get a fresh take on what a superhero struggles with? Would alcohol even make Supergirl or Superman drunk? Was the original Supergirl a heavy drinker? 
I agree with CD that movies are not providing value to the customer with little originality and a barely contained hostility to the normal audience. I could list quotes from Kathleen Kennedy and Rachael Zigler as examples but I really don't want to. The last movie I saw at the theater was Endgame and that may be the last movie I pay for. Just my opinion

replies.author: @twixraiderhm
replies.text: @Edward-op3yc ​The comics properly explain your questions, and the movie probably will too (again, assuming it'll remain faithful to the source material, which it probably will since the original creator is heavily involved in the movie's production). I'm also a bit tired of superhero movies in general since Endgame, but I do think it's unfair to write a movie off based on nothing but the trailer and the (incorrect) interpretations of an influencer. Ultimately you'll have to decide for yourself whether or not a movie provides value to you, but the only true way of knowing is to actually watch the movie yourself with an open mind. And if it turns out the movie wasn't for you then isn't that also okay? More than 50,000 movies released last year alone; not every movie is going to be for you or me, but it's probably healthier to just move on and find something more enjoyable rather than constantly cynically dunking on movies and trying to fit them into some political framework like the Drinker constantly does lol

replies.author: @justinmorgan7851
replies.text: Usually, I agree with the Drinker. I agree the dog is annoying. But overall, I think the Drinker's perspective is off here. Supergirl was born before Superman but is biologically younger because of how she traveled to Earth. Unlike Superman, Supergirl saw her world being destroyed and everyone she loved dies. Superman was just a baby when it happened and doesn't remember it. So, she has good reason to be depressed. Also, she only has powers when she's near a yellow sun, and parts of this movie take place on planets around a red sun, so she'll be a girlboss for part of the film but won't be overwhelmingly OP throughout, which should create an interesting dynamic. That's why they show her waiting for the starship bus in that scene, since she can't fly where she's at.

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text: For all the negativity around AI, it will open up opportunities for creatives to bring their own stories to life without the Hollywood machine.  Small teams will be able to produce stories themselves with modest investment.  Platforms like this one will provide the means for them to share and monetise their work.  Those that produce compelling stories will be successful, those that don't won't.  Traditional filmmakers will have to seriously up their game to compete.

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
​@TheRockerX firstly who said anything about "the average person"?  Right now creatives have to go and pitch their ideas to studio execs.  With access to AI they will have the option to  go it alone by teaming up with other creatives to bring their ideas to life at an affordable investment cost instead of needing millions in studio funds.

Secondly there are already AI short films being produced.  I watched a 25 minute one just the other day, a sci-fi tale made using motion capture and AI that starred the actress from Alita: Battle Angel.  It's happening.

The big corporations for all their money have been churning out poor quality movies in recent years that have been losing money at the box office.  Cinema as we know it is dying.  Online platforms and streaming services are the future and ones like YouTube are open to everyone as a means to distribute their videos and make money from them.

There will be a lot of dross, but quality will rise to the top.  The corporations will have smaller rivals and they'll need to seriously up their game.

Ultimately with AI levelling the playing field on budgets and production values the stories themselves will be back taking centre stage.

replies.text:
​@norbertfenz3506what part of the trailer implies that Kara hates all men? Or that she's oblivious to her own flaws? 

If you read the comic this is adapting you'd know that Kara is incredibly introspective. She drinks because of her trauma from witnessing the slow destruction of Krypton over the course of months, not because of anything related to men.

If you think James Gunn should be listening to what "audiences" want, then you should be more than happy with this teaser. Because from what's shown it's going to be a faithful adaptation of the most popular Supergirl storyline ever. Unless of course you don't actually care about the comics and just hate that a woman character has flaws.

A/B testing can eliminate anything else that might be unexpected and take a little while for audiences to appreciate. Slop is exactly the right word: it's a mad libs approach where they recycle anything that had traction in the past, blindly chasing an algorithm that rewards immediate first-glance reactions.

replies.author: @Gribold
replies.text:
@Meg4Rex You’re telling me my account of my own enjoyment is wrong. That’s not how opinions work.
You can disagree with, for example, “Superman 2025 is a bad movie”; you don’t get to dispute “I didn’t enjoy it.”

You then ascribe my lack of interest to nostalgia, despite knowing nothing about me. That’s speculation, not insight.

I dislike many contemporary films because they’re corporate products, not works of art. They're defined and controlled by committees and accountants, not auteurs. It's the commoditisation I object to not the despair that nostalgia brings.

In other words, don't presume to lecture me on what I should or shouldn't feel about something I am invited to pay for.

replies.author: @BlargeMan
replies.text: Actually, that is Supergirl in at least one comic run, and I think it makes her an interesting character. Certainly more interesting than just being a female Superman clone, and having them be cousins with identical powers yet such different life experiences and outlooks is an interesting juxtaposition. It has the potential for some really emotionally rich storytelling and writing, but that's something that requires extremely competent writers to not make it feel ham-fisted.

replies.author: @alexbrennan6285
replies.text:
He’s wrong on all his assumptions because it’s an adaptation of a comic mini series that’s been out for a couple of years already so how did he nail it exactly? 
“Wacky” the story is dark, “world ending conflict” it’s a personal revenge tale, “group of misfits and outcasts” 1 other person and “learn to work together” Kara and Ruthye are on the same page and have a common goal. Not to mention the “snarky humour Gunn worked in” are lines from the book as Tom King is working on the movie and the DCU.
Will is a lazy grifter who relies on his audience not caring about the details of what he’s actually ranting on about as long as it sounds plausible enough to confirm their biased opinions.
Worst of all Will creating the narrative that she’s “going through a mid life crisis at 23 and how that must be difficult whilst being super-powered and good looking”. He thinks you’re all fools as he doesn’t even need to bother fact checking if he’s right about the plot because he knows it doesn’t matter he’s completely wrong why her life is a wreck. She has a very good reason why she’s in the state we find her.
Yeah, he totally nailed it mate.

replies.author: @AB3Aaric
replies.text:
Well the context of that is definitely needed. The idea was not that Superman was blamed, so much as the idea that the destruction level was far beyond what anyone on earth at the time could even fathom. Superman was an unstoppable force and even other beings just like him couldn't defeat him.  That level of power had nothing to keep it in check and therefore he needed to either leave or they needed a contingency (as Batman always makes) to defeat or kill him. 

Batman had also been having nightmares and wild dreams and visions of the future involving a dark Superman. He saw that earth stood no chance if this alien had one bad day and took it out on the world. (See Injustice Superman for reference. Lol). This also came to a head when Superman pushed his way into Batman's affairs and essentially threatened him.... At that point, Batman felt he had no other choice but to do everything in his power to protect the world when no one else could. Sure, the movie was definitely not done the best and the entire last 25 minutes was terrible, but the premise holds up until that point.

replies.author: @cidadaoanonimo6903
replies.text: Sorry, but Gunn's Superman was a horrible movie for me and my ENTIRE family. The story is poorly developed. Several jokes at moments where there SHOULDN'T be jokes. All that nonsense about the evil biological parents (which Gunn literally puts in all his movies). We barely have scenes about the war and the population's rejection of Superman, things that are CENTRAL plot points. And most importantly, that WASN'T Superman, it was Gunn dressing up as him. Superman in the movie makes references to "punk-rock" because that's something Gunn likes. Gunn changed Krypto's breed to look like HIS OWN dog. Superman gets kicked in the balls during the final fight because that's something Gunn finds funny. And even Superman's biological parents became evil in this movie, most likely because Gunn HIMSELF has some unresolved issue with his parents.

replies.author: @darcebader2126
replies.text: ​​​@cidadaoanonimo6903You sound like you're parroting someone I've heard. I'm not saying it was great, but its the most fun superman movie. The most relatable he's ever been. In Man of Steal, he barely seemed human. Ik he isnt, just saying.

replies.author: @cidadaoanonimo6903
replies.text: ​@darcebader2126 Where does Superman "not seem human" in Man of Steel? He has doubts and fears about who he is, shows disappointment in himself when he fails, smiles when he's with people he loves, feels anger when Zod tries to hurt his mother, becomes desperate when Zod threatens to kill people, and is saddened when he has to kill him. Where is the lack of humanity in that? I dare say that Superman's humanity in Man of Steel is far less artificial than Gunn's Superman.

replies.author: @darcebader2126
replies.text: ​​@cidadaoanonimo6903i distinctly remembering him questioning whether humanity was deserving of his protection. He was protrayed as higher than and it was off putting

replies.author: @cidadaoanonimo6903
replies.text: ​@darcebader2126 Superman has ALWAYS been this way; he is NOT human, and he knows it. He doesn't get sick, he doesn't get hungry, and he doesn't have to go through any of the problems that normal humans go through. His genetics make him extremely privileged compared to people, and he has NEVER tried to deny this or make himself less than that. He IS superior to humans, and he knows it, but still, instead of ruling the planet with an iron fist, he chooses to help people. This has always been the issue behind Superman. He is someone who is superior to humans, but his power hasn't made him evil, and this is also the main basis for Lex's hatred towards him, because Lex can't accept that a superior being isn't, consequently, evil.

replies.author: @darcebader2126
replies.text: ​@cidadaoanonimo6903The problem with superman is that he is so powerful that he has to be near perfect. James Gunn did the best ast humanizing him, by making him struggle with his purpose on earth and also making powers on earth a threat to him, instead of other gods from across the galaxy.
